pages.intro: You've completed 12 activities and become an algorithm thinker. Before you close this book, take a moment to look back — that's what real programmers do after every project! Reflection is where growth happens.

section_1.instruction: Fill in whatever comes to mind. There are no wrong answers — just YOUR answers!

prompts.text: The most SURPRISING thing I learned was...
The activity I found HARDEST was Activity ___ because...
The activity I LOVED the most was Activity ___ because...
One thing I want to KEEP THINKING about is...

section_2.instruction: Design your own algorithm as a picture! It can be a flowchart, a decision tree, a list of steps — however you want to show it. Pick something you want to make an algorithm for (a game, a chore, a routine — anything!).

drawing_box.instruction_in_box: Sketch your algorithm here — use boxes, arrows, diamonds, or whatever helps you show it!

section_3.instruction: Now that you're an algorithm thinker, where will you take this skill? Circle any (or all!) of these next steps you're interested in — or invent your own!
options: Try a coding language like Scratch or Python
Solve more logic puzzles (Sudoku, chess, escape rooms)
Build your own workbook for a younger sibling
Design a game or app idea
Teach a friend one concept from this book
Look for algorithms everywhere in real life

content.body:
Thank you for taking this journey! When I designed this workbook, my hope was that you'd come away not just knowing what an algorithm is, but feeling like a person who THINKS with algorithms. If any part of this book made you say "huh, cool!" — that's the goal.

Now go find more problems worth solving.

pages.intro: This workbook teaches computational thinking through 12 unplugged activities aligned to CSTA K-12 CS Standards. Each activity builds on the previous ones, and every activity has both a Basic (ages 8-10) and Challenge (ages 10-12) version for natural differentiation. Use this guide to plan lessons, track standards coverage, or extend the material.

rows: Understand that algorithms are step-by-step instructions
Recognize that order matters in algorithms
Use loops and nested loops in pseudocode
Identify and fix bugs in instruction sets
Execute a named sorting algorithm unplugged
Compare two algorithms for the same task
Identify repeating, growing, and rule-based patterns

standards.description: Compare multiple algorithms for the same task.
Create programs that use algorithms to solve problems.
Create programs that include sequences, events, loops, and conditionals.
Decompose problems into smaller, manageable subproblems.
Modify, remix, or incorporate portions of an existing program into one's own work.
Give attribution when using ideas and creations of others.
Test and debug a program or algorithm to ensure it runs as intended.

tips: Introduce each activity by reading the "How It Works" section aloud. This front-loads the key concept before kids attempt the exercises.
Let kids attempt the Basic version independently, then transition to Challenge only if they're ready. Not every kid needs to do the Challenge — that's the point of differentiation.
Use the Bonus activities for extension work, homework, or "early finisher" tasks. Many are hands-on or social.
Return to earlier activities anytime — the material is designed to compound, so review is genuinely valuable, not remedial.
Discussion prompts in each Teacher/Parent Note give you conversation-starters ready to go.

extensions.title: Coding Playground
extensions.description: Introduce kids to Scratch (scratch.mit.edu) or Code.org's free courses. The concepts they learned here — loops, conditionals, sequences, decomposition — map directly to visual coding blocks. Most kids will feel instantly at home.

extensions.title: Algorithm Journaling
extensions.description: Have kids identify one algorithm they encounter each day for a week — a school-day routine, a game strategy, a household process. Discuss what tools it uses (sequence, loop, conditional). This turns pattern recognition into a lifelong habit.

extensions.title: Teach-Back Project
extensions.description: Have your child teach ONE activity from this workbook to a younger sibling, friend, or classmate. Teaching is the deepest form of learning — and it's the same activity that closes this workbook (Activity 12 Bonus).

extensions.title: Design Their Own
extensions.description: Encourage kids to design a workbook page of their own — inventing a new activity that teaches an algorithm concept. Kids who can design lessons truly understand the material.

closing.text: Questions or feedback? We'd love to hear from you at yusufahmed.sdet@gmail.com or on LinkedIn (linkedin.com/in/md-yusuf-ahmed).
